facts_disclosed: Immigrated from St. Petersburg, Russia at age 14 with her mother

turns.content: do you know anything about adapting recipes for Canadian ingredients when the original recipe is very specifically Russian? I'm trying to recreate something from my childhood and the ingredients here are just... not the same

That's a common challenge with any cuisine that relies on region-specific ingredients. Russian cooking especially has some things that are hard to replicate -- certain dairy products, specific grain flours, even the water can affect dough. What recipe are you working on, and what ingredients are giving you trouble?

it's a smetannik, a sour cream cake my family used to make. the smetana here in Montreal is not the same as what we had back home. it's thinner, less tangy. I've tried every brand at the Russian store on Decarie and nothing tastes right

You're right that North American sour cream is generally thinner and milder than Russian smetana. A few tricks that might help: you could strain regular sour cream through cheesecloth overnight to thicken it, then add a tiny bit of lemon juice or citric acid to boost the tang. Some people also mix full-fat sour cream with a small amount of creme fraiche to get closer to that richness. Have you tried any of those approaches?

the cheesecloth thing is smart actually, I haven't tried that. the creme fraiche mix I've done but it's still missing something. I think part of it is that I'm chasing a taste memory and maybe no ingredient will match what I remember from when I was a kid in Petersburg

That's a really honest observation. Taste memories are powerful and they're tied to so much more than just the ingredients -- the kitchen, the person who made it, how old you were. Sometimes the gap between memory and reality can't be closed with a recipe adjustment. How long ago did you leave Petersburg?

I was 14 when we left. just me and my mother. she packed two suitcases and we got on a plane and that was it. no French, no English, nothing. Montreal was another planet. I remember landing at Dorval in January and thinking I was going to freeze to death even though Petersburg winters are worse, because at least there I knew where I was going

That's an incredibly brave thing your mother did -- picking up everything with a teenager and starting over in a completely new country with no common language. January in Montreal as your first impression of Canada must have been overwhelming. How did you both manage those early days without French or English?

you just figure it out. mama cleaned houses, I went to school and understood nothing for months. but here's the funny thing -- the kitchen is where I started to connect. I got a part-time job washing dishes at a bistro when I was 16 and the cooks spoke French and I learned kitchen French before I learned regular French. "oui chef" before "bonjour comment ca va." the kitchen made sense when nothing else did

That's a beautiful way to find your footing -- the kitchen as a universal language. The structure of a professional kitchen, the hierarchy, the calls and responses, it all works the same regardless of what language you speak outside of it. And learning the craft language first actually probably gave you a deeper connection to cooking than most people have. Did that dishwashing job spark the whole career path?

it did, yeah. the chef there was nothing special, a small bistro, basic food. but he let me watch. and then he let me prep. and I realized this was the thing I could do better than anyone around me, because I already knew food. I already understood flavour and texture and how heat transforms things. I just didn't know I knew it until someone put a knife in my hand in a professional kitchen

It sounds like the foundation was already there from your childhood in Russia, and the bistro just gave you the professional framework to build on it. That instinct for flavour and transformation -- where did that come from originally? Family cooking?

da, my grandmother. but that's a whole other conversation and it's midnight and I'm getting sentimental which means I need to stop. the smetannik -- I'm going to try the cheesecloth method this weekend and see if straining the sour cream gets me closer. if it works I owe you one

Let the sour cream strain for at least 12 hours, and use the full-fat kind -- the low-fat won't give you the right body. And if you want to push the tang further, a half teaspoon of citric acid dissolved in a tiny bit of water mixed into the strained cream might be the missing piece. Good luck with it -- I hope it gets you closer to the memory. And whenever you want to talk about your grandmother and food, I'm here for that conversation too.
merci. honestly this whole recipe is for her anyway, even if she'll never taste it. ok bonne nuit for real this time, I have prep at 8am and I'm still awake like an idiot. spasibo

Bonne nuit! Get some rest and let that sour cream do its thing this weekend. Cooking for someone's memory is one of the most meaningful things you can do in a kitchen. Hope the smetannik turns out beautifully.
